title: Light-tunable quantum metric non-linear Hall response in Berry dipole semimetals
abstract: We investigate the effect of light on quantum metric-mediated intrinsic nonlinear Hall conductivity in Berry dipole semimetals. We discover that light induces a tunable asymmetry in the off-diagonal part of the quantum metric, which is manifested by an asymmetry in the quantum metric dipole. We show that the nonlinear response can be tuned directly by the light amplitude. In particular, we note that the direction of the nonlinear Hall signal changes when the light amplitude is increased beyond a threshold value. Light thus emerges as a promising stimulus to control the quantum geometric response in topological semimetals.
summary: This theoretical work demonstrates that applying light to Berry dipole semimetals allows for active, tunable control over their nonlinear Hall response. By inducing an asymmetry in the quantum metric via optical driving, researchers show that the direction of the resulting current can be flipped simply by increasing the light intensity past a certain threshold.
main_problem: The study investigates how light influences the intrinsic nonlinear Hall conductivity (NLHC) mediated by quantum metrics within Berry dipole semimetals.
main_result: Light induces a tunable asymmetry in the off-diagonal part of the quantum metric, leading to a controllable NLHC whose direction can reverse when the light amplitude exceeds a critical threshold.
method: The authors used theoretical calculations involving Floquet theory (Floquet-Magnus expansion) and derived expressions for the Quantum Metric Dipole (DQM) under high-frequency optical driving.
model_or_system: The system modeled is a Berry dipole semimetal (BDS), characterized by a quadratic band structure arising from two Weyl nodes at a mirror-symmetric interface. The analysis uses an effective Hamiltonian derived via optical driving.
key_observables: Nonlinear Hall conductivity (NLHC), Quantum Metric Dipole (QMD), and the off-diagonal components of the quantum metric ($G^{\Omega}_{xy}$).
important_parameters: Light amplitude ($A_0$), light frequency ($\Omega$), and chemical potential ($\mu$).
main_assumptions: The analysis relies on the high-frequency approximation for optical driving, and specific limits (like $\Omega 	o 0$) require careful handling.
figures_summary: Figures illustrate the BDS band structure, confirm the dipole nature of Berry curvature, show the emergence of light-induced asymmetry in quantum metric components ($G^{\Omega}_{xy}$), and plot the sign reversal of the DQM as a function of light amplitude.
paper_structure: The paper builds from defining the physical system (BDSs) and its Hamiltonian, through calculating the relevant geometric quantities (Berry curvature, Quantum Metric), applying optical driving via Floquet theory to derive effective Hamiltonians, and finally analyzing the resulting nonlinear transport response (NLHC/DQM).
why_interesting: The concept of using light as a direct stimulus to control fundamental quantum geometric responses like the Hall effect is highly relevant for designing novel optoelectronic topological materials.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: FIG. 1. (a) A schematic representation of our studied sys- tem. The Berry dipole semimetal (labeled as BDS), shows a quadratically dispersing band structure, in contrast to the linear bands in Weyl or Dirac materials. Here the conduction and valence bands touch each other at a point dipole node. We illuminate the BDS by circularly polarized light and an- alyze the quantum metric. Using it, we further explore the NLHC of the system in the presence of light.

FIG. 2. (a) The Berry curvature Ωxy is plotted with qx and qy keeping qz = 0.5. Ωxy does not show the dipole nature. In panel (b), we plot Ωyz with qz and qy keeping qx = 0.5. This shows a dipolar nature. This dipole nature is also visible in (c), where we plot Ωzx as a function of qz and qx, keeping qy = 0.5. Importantly, in (b) and (c), as we vary q, we clearly observe the dipole nature of the Berry curvature.
FIG. 3. Components of the quantum metric, Gxx (solid blue), Gyy (red dashed), and Gzz (green dot dashed) are plotted with q = p
FIG. 4. (a) The diagonal component of the quantum metric, Gzz, plotted with q = p

FIG. 5. (a) Plot of the integrand vyGxx −vxGyx with qy and qz taking qx = 0.23. (b) The same integrand, (vyGxx −vxGyx), is plotted in three-dimensions as a function of qx, qy, and qz. In panel (a), it is evident that although the patterns look symmetric, they are not identical, which is discernible from the central white structures. In panel (b), the asymmetric pattern is clearly visible in the distribution around the qz = 0 plane.
